The Biggest JavaScript Myth Isn’t About this
Ask ten JavaScript developers to explain the event loop, and you’ll probably hear words like call stack, Web APIs, microtasks, macrotasks, and task queues.
Most explanations quickly turn into diagrams with arrows pointing everywhere.
Ironically, those diagrams often make one of JavaScript’s simplest ideas feel impossibly complicated.
The event loop is not a magical scheduler.
It isn’t an intelligent traffic controller.
It doesn’t predict what your application is trying to do.
It follows an incredibly small set of rules — over and over again.
The problem isn’t that the event loop is complicated.
The problem is that those simple rules produce behavior that becomes surprisingly dangerous as applications grow.
Once you understand the few rules it actually follows, countless “random” bugs suddenly become completely predictable.
JavaScript Doesn’t…
